what_it_does: Registers a single MCP tool named runs that exposes a read-only index of past engine runs. It consolidates list and find semantics behind an op selector, delegates to existing Suite methods, and returns run metadata including run:// addresses.
observations: The module docstring is mostly design rationale, explaining why list_runs and find_runs were folded together and why get_state/get_results/get_events were left out.
A single decorated MCP tool function runs is nested inside register and returned.
The op parameter is typed as Literal['list','find'] and validated against a local OPS tuple.
Optional string filters default to empty strings and are converted to None before calling Suite methods.
since defaults to -1 meaning all events; limit defaults to 50.
The tool is read-only: it explicitly does not resolve, approve, or dispatch runs.
It relies on the live suite.ENGINE_RUN_OPS registry rather than hardcoding valid run_kind values.
The return value prefixes the suite result with the requested op key.
standouts: Heavy inline architectural commentary justifying the tool boundary and the op-selector consolidation.
Explicit refusal to fold graph-state and global-trajectory resources into the same tool to avoid a god-tool.
Registry-is-truth pattern: run_kind validation is delegated to the Suite, not duplicated in the tool.
conventions: Docstring-first design with long explanatory docstrings at module and function level.
Nested tool registration pattern: register(mcp, suite) defines and returns the tool.
Reuse-don't-parallel: wraps existing Suite.list_runs and Suite.find_runs instead of adding a new engine.
Parameter normalization: empty string means 'no filter' and is mapped to None.
concerns: role is accepted in the signature for both ops but only used when op='find', which may confuse callers.
since=-1 is a magic sentinel whose meaning is only explained in the docstring.
There is no visible error handling around the Suite method calls.
The long docstring may consume significant context window for a thin wrapper.
notes: A design-comment-heavy MCP tool file that deliberately keeps the run-index surface small by refusing to fold unrelated graph/trajectory resources into the same tool.
questions: What exact fields do suite.list_runs and suite.find_runs return?
How is the since cursor computed and ordered in the underlying event log?
Is role matching case-sensitive or normalized by the Suite?
How does the @mcp.tool() decorator expose the nested function to callers?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is exactly a consolidated, read-only run discovery tool as described.
apparent_themes: MCP design principle and resource-oriented tool boundaries
intention_signals: It is trying to make prior engine runs discoverable and chainable for agents while enforcing a narrow, resource-coherent tool boundary.
novelty: The extensive inline architectural rationale and explicit 'what was not folded here' boundary list, plus the op-selector consolidation of two previously separate query faces.
contribution: Provides a bounded, read-only run-index tool with filterable list/find semantics and clear documentation of which related resources belong elsewhere.
summary_for_embedding: mcp_face/tools/runs.py registers the read-only runs MCP tool that lists and finds past engine runs via an op selector, delegating to Suite methods and excluding unrelated graph and trajectory resources.
intention_for_embedding: To give agents a discoverable, newest-first run index with run:// addresses so outputs can be reused as inputs without prior knowledge of addresses.

symbols.does: Registers the runs MCP tool with the server.
symbols.description: Top-level registration hook that takes the mcp server object and suite instance, defines the runs tool function decorated with @mcp.tool(), and returns it so the tool is wired into the server.

symbols.does: Dispatches list or find queries against the suite's run event log.
symbols.description: The MCP tool implementation that validates the op selector, normalizes optional string filters by converting empty strings to None, calls suite.list_runs or suite.find_runs, and returns a dictionary keyed by op plus the suite result.
